translatedText: Got a job at an insurance company in Prague.
November 1, 1907
Joined the Assicurazioni Generali insurance company.
Resigned from the Generali insurance company.
Joined the Worker's Accident Insurance Institute for the Kingdom of Bohemia.
Received an asbestos factory business proposal from Karl Hermann, the husband of his sister Elli.
Met Felice Bauer at Max Brod's house.
Began writing The Man Who Disappeared (later retitled Amerika) and The Metamorphosis.
Received a medal for inventing a safety helmet.
Began writing In the Penal Colony and The Trial.
Finished the short story collection A Country Doctor.
Diagnosed with tuberculosis.
Broke up with Felice Bauer.
Retired from the insurance company.
Began writing The Castle.
Moved to Berlin to focus on writing.
June 3, 1924
Died at the Kierling tuberculosis sanatorium near Vienna, Austria.
December 31, 1924
Buried in the Jewish cemetery in the Žižkov district of Prague.
Invention of the Safety Helmet and Contribution to Mortality Reduction
Received a medal in 1912 for inventing a safety helmet, contributing to reducing the mortality rate in steel mills in the Kingdom of Bohemia to 25 per 1,000 people.
Authorship of Major Literary Works
Wrote numerous literary works, including The Metamorphosis, The Trial, The Castle, The Man Who Disappeared, In the Penal Colony, and A Country Doctor.
In what language did Kafka write his works?
Except for a few letters written in Czech to Milena Jesenská, most were written in Prague German.
How did Kafka want his works to be handled?
In a will sent to his friend facing death, he requested that all his writings be burned.
How did Kafka's works become known after his death?
His friend Max Brod went against Kafka's will and oversaw the publication of many works he possessed, making them known worldwide.
February 2, 1882
Born as the first son to John Stanislaus Joyce and Mary Jane Murray at 41 Brighton Square West, Rathgar, Dublin, Ireland.
Published the poetry collection Chamber Music.
Published the short story collection Dubliners.
Published the play Exiles.
Published the novel A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man.
Published the novel Ulysses.
Published the novel Finnegans Wake.
January 13, 1941
Died.
Ulysses
A famous novel that contributed to the avant-garde of modernism.
Finnegans Wake
A highly complex sequel to Ulysses.
Dubliners
A collection of short stories depicting the cynical and melancholic lives of Dubliners in the early 20th century and the harsh realities of society at the time.
A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man
A semi-autobiographical novel.
Chamber Music
A collection of poetry published in 1907.
Exiles
A play published in 1915.
What kind of writer was James Joyce?
A writer from Dublin, Ireland, who worked in various fields including novels, poetry, and plays, and is recognized as a leading novelist of modernism.
What are his representative works?
His famous novels include Ulysses, Finnegans Wake, Dubliners, and A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man.
What was the main background that influenced his literary world?
His spiritual and fictional world is deeply rooted in his hometown of Dublin, Ireland, and Dublin provided much of the themes and settings for his novels.
What was James Joyce's stance on the Catholic religion?
Through his Jesuit education, he realized the injustice and cruelty of the priests, which alienated him from the Catholic religion and led him to depict it critically in his works.
Born on December 22 in Lucca, Tuscany, Italy.
Entered the Milan Conservatory at the age of 23 and studied under Ponchielli and other teachers.
Graduated from the Milan Conservatory at the age of 26.
Presented his debut opera the year after graduation, receiving favorable reviews.
The premiere of the opera Manon Lescaut, based on the original work by Abbé Prévost, was a great success, establishing his position as an opera composer.
Began his era of masterpieces by presenting the opera La Bohème.
Presented the opera Tosca.
Presented the opera Madama Butterfly.
Presented the opera La fanciulla del West.
Approaching the age of 61, he presented the triptych of operas Il tabarro, Suor Angelica, and Gianni Schicchi.
Died of a heart attack in Brussels on November 29, just before his 66th birthday, where he had gone for throat cancer surgery.
Securing Worldwide Fame
Holds worldwide fame as the greatest Italian opera composer since Giuseppe Verdi.
Success of the Opera Manon Lescaut
Decisively established his position as an opera composer with the great success of the opera Manon Lescaut.
Opening of the Masterpiece Era
Opened his era of masterpieces starting with the opera La Bohème in 1896, and presented representative works such as Tosca and Madama Butterfly.
Composition of Widely Known Arias
Composed widely known arias such as "O mio babbino caro" from Gianni Schicchi and "Nessun Dorma" from Turandot.
Utilization of German and Italian Operatic Techniques
Evaluated as one of the composers who successfully used both German and Italian operatic techniques.
How is Puccini evaluated as an opera composer?
He holds worldwide fame as the greatest Italian opera composer since Giuseppe Verdi, and is evaluated as one of the composers who successfully used both German and Italian operatic techniques.
What are Puccini's representative opera works?
His representative works include La Bohème, Tosca, and Madama Butterfly.
What are the widely known arias from Puccini's operas?
"O mio babbino caro" from Gianni Schicchi and "Nessun Dorma" from Turandot are widely known.
When did Puccini die?
He died of a heart attack in Brussels on November 29, 1924, just before his 66th birthday.
Who completed Puccini's unfinished opera Turandot?
The unfinished work Turandot was completed by Franco Alfano, a junior classmate from the Milan Conservatory who was acquainted with Puccini.
Born in Paris, France.
His father, François Baudelaire, died when he was 6 years old.
Was sent to Kolkata, India, at the behest of his stepfather, but only went as far as Réunion Island before returning to France.
Although he had no published writings yet, he was known as a dandy in the art world.
Squandered half of his inheritance from his biological father and asked his mother for money for his success as a writer.
Published his first art criticism work, Salon of 1845.
Wrote his second Salon review, earning a reputation as an advocate and critic of Romanticism.
The novella La Fanfarlo was published.
Participated in the revolution.
Suffered from deteriorating health, heavy debts, and irregular creative output.
His first and most famous collection of poetry, Les Fleurs du mal (The Flowers of Evil), was published.
Attempted to leave Paris and settle in Honfleur, but it did not go well.
Continued his writing activities, such as translating Edgar Allan Poe's The Narrative of Arthur Gordon Pym of Nantucket and writing reviews.
Left Jeanne Duval when another of her lovers moved in with her.
